feedback.text: The Digital Fairness Act should encompass the protection of videogames from destruction. In an era where art, culture, and entertainment are increasingly delivered through digital platforms, videogames stand as one of the most significant cultural artifacts of the 21st century. Yet, unlike books, films, or musicwhich are preserved, archived, and accessible even decades latervideogames are regularly erased from existence by corporate decision-making. The Digital Fairness Act, or any future legislation meant to safeguard consumer rights in the digital age, must address this destructive trend. Many games companies, including, notably, the France-based Ubisoft Entertainment SA, and others have increasingly relied on live service models and persistent online requirements not only to monetize more aggressively but also to retain total control over a games lifecycle. Once the game's profitability dips below some arbitrary internal threshold, they pull the plugkilling access, eliminating purchases, and in many cases, wiping out games people paid full price for. This is not just a violation of consumer trust, its a violation of cultural preservation and digital ownership. You dont buy a game anymore; you rent it until a boardroom decides its no longer worth maintaining. Protecting videogames from this kind of destruction isnt just about nostalgia or entitlementits about preserving our cultural memory. Imagine if classic films or novels had self-destruct timers. Would we allow The Godfather or 1984 to disappear just because a studio executive declared them no longer financially viable? Videogames deserve the same respect. Whether its single-player experiences that inexplicably require constant online verification or multiplayer games with no fallback LAN or P2P options, developers and publishers must be held accountable for ensuring that games remain accessible after commercial support ends. Therefore, the Digital Fairness Act should mandate the right to offline functionality or community-run server options after official shutdowns. At the very least, publishers should be required to unlock game code or provide preservation solutions for the public domain once they choose to discontinue support. Anything less is corporate-sanctioned vandalism of art, commerce, and history. Consumers are not just paying for temporary accesstheyre buying a piece of digital culture. And its time the law reflected that.
